name: Luiza Lian: Cartografia do Sagrado
description:
Luiza Lian is a São Paulo artist whose music braids MPB, electronic production, and Afro-Brazilian spirituality into a living cartography of the sacred. Across four albums — her self-titled debut (2016), Oyá Tempo (2017), Azul Moderno (2018), and 7 Estrelas | quem arrancou o céu? (2023) — she maps where the orixás live inside modern Brazilian life: in crowded buses, empty refrigerators, buried rivers, and torn skies.

She is not performing religion. She is channeling it — the way a river channels rain.

This grammar holds each song as a card. Portuguese lyrics sit beside Yoruba English translations — not word-for-word but spirit-for-spirit transmissions that carry cultural and spiritual context across languages. The meta-emergences reveal deeper constellations: the Yabás (feminine orixás), Sacred Waters (Oxum & Yemanjá), the Crossroads (Exu & Pomba Gira), and Storms of Transformation (Oyá/Iansã).

Produced by Charles Tixier. Co-produced by Tim Bernardes (O Terno). Released on selo RISCO.

attribution.note: Original music and Portuguese lyrics by Luiza Lian. Yoruba English translations by PlayfulProcess. Produced by Charles Tixier, co-produced by Tim Bernardes. Released on selo RISCO.

Ônibus Lotado

Eu todo dia de manhã pego um ônibus lotado
Prenso o coração na porta e me sinto apaixonado...
bate bate porta
bate bate porta
meu coração    
tem neblinas como todas as manhãs
que pena você foi embora
e eu perdi a hora de instalar meu imã
bate porta coração
preciso de um corpo colado
todo dia de manhã
um ônibus lotado
sections.Yoruba English:
The crowded bus—
bodies pressed against bodies,
stranger sweat mixing with stranger sweat,
the democracy of rush hour.

Ônibus lotado:
where the city forces intimacy
on people who will never know each other's names.

Exu rides this bus.
He loves the crossroads of bodies,
the intersection of lives
that will never intersect again.

You are never alone
in the crowded places.
You are held
whether you want to be or not.
sections.Reflection: Where are you held by strangers? What intimacy do you resist that life keeps offering?

sections.Yoruba English:
Crown of flowers—
what we place on the dead
and on the bride.

Coroa de flores:
the beauty we offer
at beginnings and endings,
as if flowers could soften
the terror of transformation.

Oxum wears flowers in her hair.
So does the corpse.
Beauty does not distinguish
between the living and the gone—
it adorns whatever shows up.
sections.Reflection: What endings deserve your flowers? What beginnings have you crowned with beauty?

sections.Yoruba English:
The talker—
falador, the one who speaks,
the one who cannot stop speaking,
the one for whom silence is unbearable.

Words are Exu's children.
They run ahead of meaning,
they play tricks,
they open and close doors.

Falador:
are you speaking to connect
or speaking to avoid?
Are your words bridges
or are they walls
pretending to be bridges?
sections.Reflection: When do your words connect and when do they hide you? What are you afraid silence might reveal?

Linda, Linda 

Por falta de carinho ou maré
Por falta de cachimbo ou café
Viciado
Desejoso
Assustado de fascinação

Na busca de um espelho qualquer
Na fúria de um destino melhor
Distraído pisou
Iludiu, despedaçou o coração de uma pobre mulher

Linda, Linda!
Receba estas flores mas não saia do lugar
Musa, muda, maravilhosa!
Pouca é a prosa ao te ver passar

Voa, fica na lua

sections.Yoruba English:
Beautiful, beautiful—
the word repeated
becomes incantation.

Linda, linda:
when you say it twice
you are not just describing.
You are insisting.
You are casting a spell.

Oxum looks in her mirror
and does not doubt.
She says linda, linda
to her own reflection
until the reflection believes.
sections.Reflection: What beauty have you been afraid to name twice? What needs your insistent blessing?

sections.Yoruba English:
The light of the valley—
where light gathers
in the low places,
where illumination pools
like water finding its level.

A luz do velle:
the valley is not darkness.
The valley is where light learns
to be soft,
to be held,
to rest before climbing again.

You do not always have to be
on the mountain.
The valley has its own knowing.
sections.Reflection: What does the light in your valleys teach you? What wisdom lives in your low places?

sections.Português:
Escuta Zé 

Escuta Zé,
você me cansa com seus medos
e essa ânsia pelo arremedo.
Você está morto
com o pavor que seu corpo inspira.
Você quer mais, mas
a flor que você exala lhe pira
e iguala, me escuta, Zé, , aos animais.
Você se contenta com a alegria com data dos carnavais.
Você quer correr pelos campos numa exaltação da vida
e consegue no máximo alegorias de amor na Avenida.
Você só ama o amante na cama pela televisão enquadrado
E as muitas putas com coração das multidões de Jorge amado.
Você quer ser livre, você quer saída,
Mas na verdade tem asco de sua própria liberdade
Quando é de fato vivida.
Escuta Zé, você me enjoa
Com essa vida que você leva a toa
Falando para os ouros que ela é boa
Quando é de fato ferida.
Seu presente para o amor é o sonho
Ou a coroa de espinho;
Você morrerá cedo, Zé
Por que seu desejo é mesquinho.
sections.Yoruba English:
Listen, Zé—
the common name,
the everyman,
the one who is all of us.

Escuta:
the command that is also invitation.
Listen, ordinary one.
Listen, you who think you are not special.
Listen, because this message
is exactly for you.

Zé—José—
the carpenter's name,
the dreamer's name,
the one who almost got left out
of the holy story
but stayed anyway.
sections.Reflection: What message keeps trying to reach your ordinary self? What have you dismissed because you thought it wasn't for someone like you?

sections.Yoruba English:
Garden—
the place where wildness
and tending meet.

Jardim:
not wilderness,
not concrete,
but the negotiation between them.

Ossaim tends the plants
that heal and harm.
Every garden is pharmacy.
Every flower is medicine or poison
depending on the dose.

What are you growing?
What are you weeding?
The garden does not happen
without your hands in the dirt.
sections.Reflection: What garden are you tending? What grows there that you didn't plant, and what have you been weeding that wanted to stay?

sections.Yoruba English:
Mississippi / Light—
the great river of another America
meeting the Portuguese word for illumination.

Mississippi:
the water that divides and connects,
the boundary that is also a road.
Luz:
what makes the water visible.

Two words, two worlds:
the blues river
and the Brazilian light.
They meet in her mouth
and become one song
about how far water travels
to find the sea.
sections.Reflection: What distant rivers meet in you? What lights from different worlds converge to make your particular brightness?

sections.Yoruba English:
Moon—
luna, lua,
the silver that rules the night,
the face that changes
while staying the same.

Yemanjá's mirror in the sky.
Every woman's clock
before clocks existed.

Luna:
she who pulls the tides
of ocean and blood,
who teaches that fullness
and emptiness
are the same cycle
seen from different nights.

You are always whole.
Even when you show only a sliver.
sections.Reflection: What phase are you in? What wholeness exists in you even when only a sliver shows?

sections.Yoruba English:
The tucum palm weaves itself through my fingers—
black seeds strung on the thread of resistance.
Everything that survives the burning
becomes jewelry.

The ancestors wore this darkness on their wrists
not as mourning but as morning—
each bead a small sun
that refused to set.

I am learning to adorn myself
with what cannot be taken.
The forest floor remembers
every seed that fell and rose.
sections.Reflection: What in you has survived the burning? What darkness do you wear as adornment?

sections.Yoruba English:
There is light, there is light—

The moisture that gathers on leaves at dawn

Every seat an altar
waiting for its saint.

The wood remembers
everyone who came before you.
You are not alone in your sitting.

Manada—

sections.Yoruba English:
Is it in her that one lives?
Is it in her that one dwells?

The question spirals:
Is the body the house?
Is the song the house?
Is the mother the house?
Is the land the house?

The answer keeps changing

must breathe with your breathing.

Morar—to dwell, to live, to inhabit.

You are always arriving

sections.Yoruba English:
I am here—
the simplest sentence,
the hardest truth.

Eu estou aqui.
Not 'I was here' or 'I will be'—
I am here now,
in this body,
in this breath,
in this moment that is already gone
but I caught it long enough to say:

I am here.
The ancestors can find me.
The future can find me.
I am not hiding.
I am not gone.
Eu estou aqui—
and that is enough.
sections.Reflection: Can you say 'I am here' and mean it? What would it take to stop hiding?

dancing in the blood that dances.

the syncretism of survival,
the disguise that became a doorway.

When the slavers banned the orixás,
the orixás wore the saints like masks
and kept dancing.

Epa hey, Iansã!
You who are also Barbara,
you who carry lightning
whether they call you saint or spirit—
your power does not need the right name.
It needs only to be called.

not the blue of the old masters,
not the blue of the sea your grandmothers crossed,

the color of now,
the sadness that is not your mother's sadness

from old blues.

The sky stays the same
but we keep painting it.

you need to get through this.

the pain is making you
into a weapon of peace.

sections.Yoruba English:
I am here—
the simplest sentence,
the hardest truth.

Eu estou aqui.
Not 'I was here' or 'I will be'—
I am here now,
in this body,
in this breath,
in this moment that is already gone
but I caught it long enough to say:

I am here.
The ancestors can find me.
The future can find me.
I am not hiding.
I am not gone.
Eu estou aqui—
and that is enough.
sections.Reflection: Can you say 'I am here' and mean it? What would it take to stop hiding?

sections.Yoruba English:
Desabriga—
she who has no shelter,
she who the roof could not hold,
she who the walls could not contain.

This is not only tragedy.
This is also freedom:
to be unhoused
is to be unheld
is to be released
from the small rooms
everyone else fits into.

The sky is also a roof.
The horizon is also walls.
Desabriga—
maybe you were too big
for the shelters they built.
Maybe you are building
a shelter the size of the world.
sections.Reflection: What shelter have you outgrown? What would it mean to be held by something larger?

sections.Yoruba English:
Seven stars—
the Pleiades that sailors follow,
the sisters who fled into the sky,
the map that needs no paper.

Sete estrelas:
one for each day of creation,
one for each orixá of the week,
one for each wound that became a window.

Who tore off the sky?
Who reached up and pulled?
Maybe it was you—
reaching for stars
that were closer than you thought.

The sky is not gone.
It is in your hands now.
What will you do
with all this torn heaven?
sections.Reflection: What sky have you torn open? What will you do with the stars in your hands?

sections.Yoruba English:
Deságua—
where the river gives itself to the sea,
where the fresh surrenders to the salt,
where the journey admits it was always heading here.

Yemanjá opens her arms.
Oxum releases her grip.
The river does not die at the mouth—
it becomes something larger than itself.

Deságua:
the un-watering,
the place where water becomes more water,
where the small self pours
into the self that has no edges.

This is not ending.
This is arriving.
This is finally, finally
coming home to the ocean
that was always your mother.
sections.Reflection: Where is your river meeting the sea? What are you ready to pour yourself into?

items.name: Album 2023: 7 Estrelas | quem arrancou o ceu?

sections.Yoruba English:
Yabá means queen-mother in Yoruba. She is not one goddess — she is the principle of feminine sovereignty itself. When Luiza sings "Sou Yabá," she is not choosing one deity. She is claiming the throne that all the female orixás share: the power to create, to destroy, to transform, to hold, to release.

Oxum brings the gold. Yemanjá brings the ocean. Oyá brings the storm. Nanã brings the primordial clay. Pomba Gira brings the crossroads and desire. Together, they are not a pantheon — they are a technology of feminine power that survived slavery, colonization, and erasure.
sections.For Americans: Imagine if Beyoncé, Nina Simone, Billie Holiday, Aretha Franklin, and Erykah Badu were not just musicians but actual spiritual forces that people pray to, make offerings to, and receive in their bodies during ceremony. That's what the Yabás are. Not metaphors. Not symbols. Living presences. And Luiza channels them all.
sections.Reflection: Which Yabá speaks loudest in your life right now? Are you in an Oxum season (beauty, diplomacy, inner gold)? An Oyá season (destruction, transformation, letting go)? A Yemanjá season (mothering, oceanic grief, vast holding)?

metadata.note: Iarinhas (little Iaras) are river spirits connected to Oxum's domain. The Iara is the Brazilian mermaid — indigenous, not European — who sings in the rivers of São Paulo that the city buried under concrete.

sections.Yoruba English:
Oxum rules the sweet waters — rivers, waterfalls, lakes. She is gold, honey, fertility, and the diplomacy that prevents war. Yemanjá rules the salt waters — the ocean, motherhood, the boundary between life and death.

When Luiza sings "Iarinhas," she is singing about the Iaras — the river spirits of indigenous Brazilian mythology. But the rivers she names (Tietê, Tapajós, Tamanduateí) are São Paulo's buried rivers, paved over by the city. The Iarinhas are still singing underneath the concrete. This is Oxum's territory — fresh water, urban and wild at once.

When she sings "Sou Yabá" and declares "eu danço nas ondas do mar" (I dance on the waves of the sea), she crosses from Oxum's rivers into Yemanjá's ocean. The movement from fresh to salt water is also the movement from life to death to rebirth.
sections.For Americans: For an American, think of the Mississippi — Robert Johnson's blues river. Now imagine that river has a goddess. And that every waterfall, every lake, every ocean has its own deity with specific songs, colors, foods, and days of the week. That's the Afro-Brazilian relationship to water. It's not metaphor. When Bahians throw flowers into the ocean on February 2nd for Yemanjá, millions of people participate. It's one of the largest religious celebrations in the Americas.
sections.Reflection: Where does your inner river meet the sea? What is the difference between your fresh-water self (intimate, golden, close) and your salt-water self (vast, deep, borderless)?

sections.Yoruba English:
Exu is the messenger. Without him, no prayer is heard, no offering received. The colonizers called him devil — he is not. He is the principle of communication, of movement, of the space between.

Pomba Gira is his feminine counterpart. She spins at the crossroads in her red dress, laughing at what scares you. She is desire without shame, power without permission.

In "Pomba Gira do Luar," Luiza enters the Jurema (an indigenous-Afro-Brazilian spiritual tradition) and finds her at the crossroads with a moonlit king. The crossroads is where you make choices. It's where transformation begins.
sections.For Americans: Robert Johnson went to the crossroads at midnight. In Afro-Brazilian tradition, you don't go there to sell your soul — you go to find your path. Exu is more like Hermes than Satan: the trickster-messenger who opens and closes doors. Pomba Gira is like a divine Josephine Baker — unapologetically sexual, fiercely free, and sacred precisely because of her refusal to be tamed.
sections.Reflection: What crossroads are you standing at? What choice keeps presenting itself? And what would Pomba Gira do — she who never asks permission?

sections.Yoruba English:
Oyá (Iansã) is the storm itself. She carries the dead to the other side. She sweeps the marketplace clean with her irukere (horsehair whisk). An entire album bears her name: Oyá Tempo — Oyá + Time — because transformation is not a moment, it is the weather of existence.

When the slavers banned the orixás, the enslaved people dressed their gods in Catholic clothes. Oyá became Santa Bárbara — patron saint of lightning. The syncretism was survival technology. Luiza sings both names because both are true.
sections.For Americans: If Beyoncé's "Lemonade" is about alchemy — turning pain into art — Oyá Tempo is about learning that you ARE the storm. Not surviving it. Being it. The wind does not resist itself.
sections.Reflection: What storm are you — not surviving — but actually being? What transformation would happen if you stopped sheltering and started blowing?

sections.Yoruba English:
Caboclos are indigenous Brazilian spirit guides who work in Umbanda and Jurema traditions. They are not orixás — they are ancestors of the land itself. The Boiadeiro (cowboy spirit) teaches direction: "always walking with the herd, you cannot look at the ground." The Marinheiro (sailor spirit) teaches balance: "in the swaying of my boat I teach you to balance."

Luiza channels these spirits alongside the orixás, weaving indigenous and African traditions together — as they have always been woven in Brazilian spiritual practice.
sections.For Americans: Imagine Native American spirit guides meeting West African deities in the body of a São Paulo electronic musician. That's what's happening. Brazil's spirituality braids indigenous, African, Catholic, and Kardecist spiritist traditions into something that exists nowhere else on Earth.
sections.Reflection: What indigenous wisdom of your own land have you forgotten? What spirits of place are singing underneath your concrete?
